coredata.dc:title: One-pot synthesis of CeO2-carbon dots with enhanced peroxidase-like activity and carbon dots for ratiometric fluorescence detection of H2O2 and cholesterol
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Zhouping
Liu, Yang
Lu, Changfang
Yue, Guizhou
Wang, Yanying
Rao, Hanbing
Zhang, Wei
Lu, Zhiwei
Wang, Xianxiang
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots-doped CeO2 (CeO2-CDs) with enhanced peroxidase-like activity and fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) were prepared by one-pot hydrothermal method in this work. The morphology, surface groups and enzymatic properties of CeO2-CDs and optical properties of CDs were studied in detail. The enzyme activity of CeO2-CDs was higher than CeO2 due to the smaller particle size. O-phenylenediamine (OPD) as peroxidase substrate can be catalyzed to fluorescent o-phenylenediamine (oxOPD) by CeO2-CDs, and the UV–vis absorption of oxOPD overlapped partly with the fluorescence emission of CDs. Hence, the fluorescence intensity of CDs would be decreased by oxOPD due to the inner filter effect. Based on this, a sensitive and selective ratiometric fluorescence method for H2O2 and cholesterol was developed. The detection ranges for H2O2 and cholesterol are 1.67 µM-2.01 mM and 1.66 µM-1.65 mM with the detection limits are 0.35 µM and 0.49 µM (3Ϭ), respectively. Moreover, this method has been successfully used to detect cholesterol in human serum.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CeO2
Doped
Carbon dots
Ratiometric fluorescence
H2O2
Cholesterol

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Peroxide-like activity and kinetic analysis of CeO2-CDs
Ratiometric fluorescence for H2O2 and cholesterol detection
Ratiometric fluorescence detection of H2O2 and cholesterol

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation of CDs and CeO2-CDs by one-pot hydrothermal method, and application as a ratiometric fluorescence for H2O2 and cholesterol detection.
SEM images and size distribution of CeO2 (a) and CeO2-CDs (b), XRD pattern of CeO2-CDs (c) and FT-IR spectrum of the CeO2 and CeO2-CDs (d).
XPS spectrum of CeO2-CDs(a), Ce 3d region(b), O1s region (c) and C1s region (d).

The peroxidase-like catalytic activity of CeO2-CDs and CeO2 with TMB as substrate were affected by temperature(a) and pH(b); Steady-state kinetic analysis of CeO2-CDs. Changing the concentrations of H2O2 with 0.33 mM TMB(c), changing the concentrations of TMB with 3.3 mM H2O2 (d) and the double-reciprocal model of the concentration for H2O2 (e) and for TMB(f).

The fluorescence emission peaks of the CDs and the CeO2-CDs+OPD+H2O2, and the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the CeO2-CDs+OPD+H2O2 (a) and fluorescence emissions of CDs, CDs+OPD, CDs+CeO2-CDs and CDs+CeO2-CDs+OPD+H2O2 (b).
The optimum detection conditions for H2O2. The concentration of CeO2-CDs (a), OPD (b), temperature (c) and pH (d).

Fluorescence spectrum of CDs and OPD incubation with different concentrations of H2O2 (a) and cholesterol (c). Inset: corresponding photographs under UV light. The curves of I560/I430 versus the H2O2 (b) and cholesterol (d) concentration.
Selectivity of a ratiomentric fluorescence for cholesterol detection.
Comparison of Vmax and Km of several nanozymes.
Detection of cholesterol in 100-fold dilution of human serum samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: One-pot synthesis of CeO2-carbon dots with enhanced peroxidase-like activity and carbon dots for ratiometric fluorescence detection of H2O2 and cholesterol

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science, Sichuan Agricultural University, Chengdu 611130, Sichuan, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Science, Sichuan Agricultural University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Science, Sichuan Agricultural University, Chengdu 611130, Sichuan, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Veterinary Medicine, Sichuan Agricultural University, Chengdu 611130, Sichuan, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Veterinary Medicine, Sichuan Agricultural University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Veterinary Medicine, Sichuan Agricultural University, Chengdu 611130, Sichuan, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The authors wish it to be known that, in their opinions, Zhouping Yang and Yang Liu should be regarded as joint First Authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Enhanced peroxidase-like activity CeO2-carbon dots and carbon dots were synthesized by one-pot hydrothermal method, and application as a ratiometric fluorescence for H2O2 and cholesterol detection.
ga1

Carbon dots-doped CeO2 (CeO2-CDs) with enhanced peroxidase-like activity and fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) were prepared by one-pot hydrothermal method in this work. The morphology, surface groups and enzymatic properties of CeO2-CDs and optical properties of CDs were studied in detail. The enzyme activity of CeO2-CDs was higher than CeO2 due to the smaller particle size. O-phenylenediamine (OPD) as peroxidase substrate can be catalyzed to fluorescent o-phenylenediamine (oxOPD) by CeO2-CDs, and the UV–vis absorption of oxOPD overlapped partly with the fluorescence emission of CDs. Hence, the fluorescence intensity of CDs would be decreased by oxOPD due to the inner filter effect. Based on this, a sensitive and selective ratiometric fluorescence method for H2O2 and cholesterol was developed. The detection ranges for H2O2 and cholesterol are 1.67 µM-2.01 mM and 1.66 µM-1.65 mM with the detection limits are 0.35 µM and 0.49 µM (3Ϭ), respectively. Moreover, this method has been successfully used to detect cholesterol in human serum.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), as an important biochemical substrate, plays an important role in biological system, biomedicine and other pathways, such as cell differentiation, proliferation and disease development [1]. It is well known that H2O2 can be produced by many enzyme-related reactions. For example, cholesterol can be catalyzed by its specific enzyme cholesterol oxidase to produce H2O2. Due to an abnormal cholesterol in blood being usually associated with many serious diseases [2], it is very important to detect H2O2 and cholesterol levels for basic research and disease diagnosis. Currently, numerous analytical methods were developed for detection of H2O2 and cholesterol simultaneously in different biological samples, including chemiluminescence [3], electroanalysis [4], colorimetric method [5] and fluorometric method [6]. Among these methods, the fluorometric method has attracted more attention because of its advantages of high sensitivity, convenience and operational simplicity. However, these fluorescence methods exclusively depended on a single signal output, resulting in low signal-to-background ratios. Fortunately, a ratiometric fluorescence method can overcome such defects, which is used to measure the fluorescence signals of different wavelengths at the same time, and the ratiometric fluorescence intensity is used to detect the analytes quantitatively [7]. This method provides the internal normalization response sum of the changes, thus reducing the false results and improving the detection accuracy.

Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have been used as a ratiometric fluorescence signal for many target analytes [8,9], owing to their unique fluorescence properties such as photostability, multicolored emissions, low-cost and good biocompatibility. Doped with other heteroatoms, such as nitrogen, phosphorus [10], sulfur [11] or metal ions [11,12], carbon dots have been changed their optoelectronic property, surface and local chemical reactivity, resulting in their photophysical properties being greatly improved. Nanozymes are nanomaterial-based artificial enzymes[13]. They have many advantages over natural enzymes, because they have high stability under harsh environmental conditions and bulk-scale synthesis economically. Since the discovery of nano-Fe3O4 in 2007 with peroxidase-like activity [14], many nanozymes have been used for broad applications, ranging from biomolecular detection to environmental protection [15,16]. To design and develop multifunctional nanomaterials with artificial enzymes have attracted more and more attention in recent years [17,18]. As multienzyme-like catalytic, nano CeO2 have showed excellent performance with simulated activity, robustness and biocompatibility [19–21]. Therefore, nano CeO2 have been used for glucose detection [22], biomimetic inhibition of biofouling [23] and antioxidants for Alzheimer’s disease [24]. The unique redox and catalytic activity of CeO2 is mainly due to the shift of ce4+↔ ce3+ and the existence of oxygen vacancy [25,26]. In order to improve the mimic enzyme activity of CeO2, many strategies to regulate the enzyme activity have been proposed, such as controlling the morphology, modifying the surface of nanomaterials and the design of vacancy-engineered nanoceria [27,28]. More surface defects can be obtained by introducing suitable heterogeneous atoms for uniform doping, thus adjusting the proportion of Ce4+and Ce3+. Multiple metal elements, such as Co [29], Mn [30], Cu [31], Fe [32], Ti [33], Pd [34], Zr [35] and Pt [36]have been doped successfully in CeO2 to change their structure. However, to the best of our knowledge, the research of CeO2 doped with CDs for sensing has not been reported.

Herein, we proposed a facile one-pot hydrothermal method for preparing fluorescence CDs and CeO2 material doped with CDs (CeO2-CDs). Compared with pure CeO2, the peroxide-like activity of CeO2-CDs has been enhanced significantly. The experiment process is depicted in Fig. 1. The fluorescence CDs and peroxidase-like CeO2-CDs were prepared from Ce (NO3)3·6H2O and L-histidine in alkaline environment by one-pot hydrothermal method. The prepared CeO2-CDs can oxidize o-phenylenediamine (OPD) to oxidized phenylenediamine (oxOPD) with a fluorescence emission at 560 nm in the presence of H2O2, and the oxOPD can decrease the fluorescence intensity of CDs due to the overlapping between UV–vis absorbance of oxOPD and the fluorescence excitation of CeO2. The relative enzyme activity of CeO2-CDs was affected by H2O2, and the enzyme reaction between cholesterol and cholesterol oxidase will produce H2O2. Based on this, a ratiometric fluorescence for H2O2 and cholesterol detection was developed.

All chemicals were analytical grade and used without further purification. Ce (NO3)3
· 6H2O, L-histidine , and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) were purchased from Ke-Long Reagent Co.,Ltd. (Chengdu China). O-Phenylenediamine (OPD), 3,3,5,5-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), cholesterol oxidase and cholesterol were purchased from Macklin Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Phosphate-buffered (PBS) was purchased from Beijing Solar-bio Science & Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Deionized water (DW) was used throughout the experimental procedures.

The fluorescence was measured on the fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-4500, Hitachi Ltd., Japan). A UV–vis spectrum was recorded with UV-A390 ultraviolet spectrometer (AOE Instruments, Shanghai, China). The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) observations were performed with a Hitachi S-4800. The chemical composition of the products was examined with X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, ESCALAB 250Xi, Thermo Fisher). Powder X-ray Diffraction (XRD) analysis was carried out on a Bruker D8 Advance diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation. FT-IR measurements were carried out by a Shimadzu 8400 S FT-IR spectrometer.

The CDs and CeO2-CDs were prepared by a one-pot hydrothermal treatment using Ce (NO3)3
· 6H2O and L-histidine as precursors according to the previous report with certain modifications [37]. Briefly, 2.17 g of Ce (NO3)3·6H2O and 3.60 g NaOH were dissolved in 40 mL of deionized water. After the mixture was kept stirring for 20 min, 0.5 g L-histidine was added and stirred for 30 min until it formed a milky slurry. The slurry solution was then placed in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 10 h. After the hydrothermal treatment, fresh white precipitates were separated by centrifugation, and the supernatant liquid was collected. The precipitates were washed with water and ethanol three times to remove impurities. The supernatant liquid was purified by filtration and dialysis over 48 h to remove unreacted reagents. At last, the powder of CeO2-CDs and CDs were obtained by a freeze-drying technique and storied in the refrigerator for further use. The undoped CeO2 were prepared according to the above method only without L-histidine .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Peroxide-like activity and kinetic analysis of CeO2-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The peroxidase-like activity was studied by investigating the capability of CeO2-CDs to oxidize TMB. Typically, 92 µL of 100 mM H2O2 and 70 µL of 5 mM TMB were added to 2.8 mL acetic acid buffer (pH 4.0). After vortex mixing for 1 min, 15 µL of 50 mg/mL CeO2-CDs was added quickly. The UV–vis absorbance spectrum at 652 nm was measured in time-scan mode. The optimum pH and temperature for peroxidase-like reaction were studied. To study the kinetic properties of TMB, the concentration of TMB was varied and H2O2 concentration was fixed. Similarly, the concentration of TMB was kept unchanged and the concentration of H2O2 was varied to investigate the kinetic properties of H2O2. The kinetic parameters were calculated based on the Michaelis-Menten equation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence for H2O2 and cholesterol detection
ce:section.ce:para: For the determination of H2O2, 50 µL various concentrations of H2O2 were separately mixed with 200 µL of 25 mM OPD and 20 µL of 50 mg/mL CeO2-CDs. The final volume reached 1.2 mL with acetic acid buffer (pH5.5). After reaction for 40 min at room temperature, the fluorescence spectrum was scanned under 370 nm excitation for quantitative analysis of H2O2. For the determination of cholesterol, two steps were carried out. Firstly, 100 µL different concentrations of cholesterol with 10 µL triton and 10 µL cholesterol oxidase (ChOX) were mixed with 200 µL buffer solution (pH 5.5), and the mixture was incubated at 37 °C for 60 min, Then, 200 µL of 25 mM OPD, 20 µL of 50 mg/mL CeO2-CDs and 660 µL acetic acid buffer (pH5.5) were added into the above reaction solutions. The fluorescence spectrum was measured according to above procedure. For determination of cholesterol concentration in human serum, cholesterol oxidase (5 µL) was added to 200 µL serum and then diluted using PBS. Finally, the fluorescence measurement was carried out by repeating above process.

The morphology and sizes of the prepared CeO2 and CeO2-CDs were observed by SEM and the results are shown in Figs. 2a and 2b. It can be seen from the SEM images that the prepared material is regular cubic in shape and well-distributed. The diameter of CeO2-CDs is around 46 nm, while the CeO2 is larger with a diameter about 242 nm. In the classical synthesis process of nano CeO2, the shape and catalytic performance of the material can be adjusted by the synthesis temperature and amount of NaOH [37]. The morphology of nano CeO2 was not affected evidently by adding of L-histidine , but the diameter is reduced by half, which may be due to the continuous “etching” of large cube crystals of CeO2 by the formation of small CDs. The total crystallinity of CeO2-CDs was investigated by X-ray diffraction spectroscopy (XRD) (Fig. 2c). All diffraction peaks are corresponding to (111), (200), (220), (311), (222), (400), (331), (420) and (422) planes characteristic of pure fluorite-type cubic structures of cerium oxide (JCPDS 34–0394) [38].

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum were used to compare the surface functional groups of CeO2 and CeO2-CDs. In the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 2d), the significantly enhanced broad peak in the wavenumber of 3413 cm−1 is attributed to characteristic vibration bands of the –OH and –NH3 group, which indicates that the CeO2-CDs have more abundant –OH and –NH3 group on the surface due to the L-histidine added as carbon source in the preparation process. The absorption band at 1599 cm−1 is assigned to free amino group and 1384 cm−1 is C–N stretching vibrations. The symmetric tensile vibration of C‒O‒C is detected at 1057 cm−1. The peak of 851 cm−1 is ascribed to the C–H bonds, and the broad absorption band within the range of 400–750 cm−1 corresponds to CeO2 stretching vibration [39]. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) technology was also adopted to analyze oxidation states of CeO2-CDs. The corresponding survey spectrum is shown in Fig. 3a. The spectrum of Ce 3d was well known to be complicated spectrum in the range of 880–920 eV, due to the hybridization of the O2p valence band and Ce 4 f level [40]. As shown in Fig. 3b, the Ce 3d spectrum consists of eight peaks which are represented by u and v, indicating the spin-orbit coupling of 3d5/2 and 3d3/2. Ce4+ ions are marked as the three pairs of peaks (v, u; v2, u2; and v3, u3) and the peaks (v1, u1) are belonged to Ce3+ ions. The results confirm there are two types of ions (Ce4+ and Ce3+) in the CeO2-CDs. The corresponding O1s spectrum is showed in Fig. 3c. The spectrum is asymmetric and can be deconvoluted into two peaks, indicating there are two oxygen species. The peaks of 529.1 eV and 531.7 eV are attributed to lattice oxygen O2- and O2 2- or OH-. In the spectrum of the C 1 s (Fig. 3d), the signals at 284.7 eV, 286.0 eV [41], 288.0 eV [42]and 289.7 eV [43] demonstrate there are C˭C/C‒C and C‒O/C‒N, C‒N, C˭O and O‒C˭O functional groups in the CeO2-CDs.

In order to evaluate the peroxidase-like activity of the CeO2-CDs and CeO2, a chromogenic TMB was used as an enzyme substrate for the peroxidase-like study. The results in Fig. 4a and 4b show that the CeO2-CDs and CeO2 have similar catalytic activities for TMB in a wide range of temperature and pH. The optimum temperature for CeO2-CDs was 30 ℃, while for undoped CeO2 it was 35 ℃,and the peroxidase-like activity enhanced obviously when the temperature was higher than 55 ℃. The effects of pH on the peroxidase-like activity of CeO2-CDs and CeO2 were measured by varying the pH from 3.0 to 7.0. They had good enzyme catalytic activity at pH 3.5, but the enzyme activity of CeO2 almost lost when pH was higher than 5.5. More importantly, the UV–vis absorbance of CeO2-CDs was higher than that of CeO2 under the same temperature and pH, which indicated that the CeO2 doped with CDs can enhance its peroxidase-like activity.

In order to understand the mechanism of peroxide-like activity, the steady state kinetics of CeO2-CDs was studied under the optimum conditions. The typical Michaelis-Menten curves of CeO2-CDs are depicted in Fig. 4c and 4d. The apparent kinetic parameters and other simulated enzyme materials are listed in Table 1. The obtained Km values of CeO2-CDs are a little smaller than those of CeO2 and larger than those of HRP for TMB. Besides, Km values are relatively smaller than those of other simulated enzyme materials for H2O2. These data indicate that the affinity between H2O2 and CeO2-CDs is stronger than others, and CeO2 doped with CDs can enhanced its peroxidase-like activity significantly. The CeO2 can facilitate the decomposition of H2O2 to produce HO- and O2 - through a series of reactions analogous to Fenton / Haber Weiss reactions as follows [44,45]:
(1)

(3)

e

O
2
+
H
+

The peroxide-like species were generated owing to the presence of more Ce3+ and oxygen vacancies. In addition, since the catalytic activities of nanomaterials for many reactions often depend on the morphology, size, surface area and pore structures, and the smaller sized materials have a larger surface-to-volume ratio to react with substrates, it was not a surprise to observe that the size of CeO2-CDs was smaller than that of CeO2 from Fig. 2a and 2b. Hence, CeO2-CDs have a more larger surface area than CeO2 and more oxygen vacancies can be formed due to CDs doping.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence detection of H2O2 and cholesterol
ce:section.ce:para: O-phenylenediamine (OPD) as an enzyme substrate can be oxidized by peroxidase-like CeO2-CDs and H2O2 to oxOPD (the oxidative product of o-phenylenediamine OPD), and the oxOPD emits fluorescence at 560 nm when excited at 370 nm ( Fig. 5a). In addition, the UV–vis absorption of oxOPD overlaps partly with the fluorescence emission of CDs, so the fluorescence of CDs would be decreased by oxOPD due to the inner filter effect. There is a little effect on the fluorescence of CDs by OPD or CeO2-CDs, and the two fluorescence peaks appear when CDs was mixed with OPD, CeO2-CDs and H2O2. (Fig. 5b). The relative fluorescence intensity of CDs and oxOPD changed with the concentration of H2O2, and the reaction of cholesterol oxidase (ChOX) with cholesterol produced H2O2 and the amount of H2O2 affected the enzyme activity of CeO2-CDs. Thus, the ratio of the fluorescence intensity of CDs and oxOPD was used for quantitative analysis of H2O2 and cholesterol.

In order to obtain the best results of this method, the detection parameters were optimized. When 1.6 mM H2O2, 0.83 mg/mL CeO2-CDs and 25 mM OPD were added into the acetic acid buffer (pH 5.5), and incubated in 30 °C for 40 min ( Fig. 6), the maximum results of I560/I430 were obtained.

Under the optimum conditions, the analytical performance of the H2O2 and cholesterol sensing system was evaluated. As shown in Fig. 7a and 7c, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs at 430 nm was decreased gradually and that of the oxOPD at 560 nm was increased gradually with the increasing concentration of H2O2 and cholesterol, respectively. Fig. 7b and 7d display good linear relationships of the intensity ratio (I560/I430) with the concentration of H2O2 and cholesterol in the ranges of 1.67 µM-2.01 mM and 1.66 µM-1.65 mM with the detection limits (LOD) estimated to be 0.35 µM and 0.49 µM (3Ϭ), respectively. Meanwhile, the selectivity of this method for the cholesterol was investigated. The various biological relevant interferents were measured in solution separately. As shown in Fig. 8, the interferents have no influence on I560/430, which indicates that the ratiometric fluorescence sensor has excellent selectivity toward cholesterol.

In order to verify the feasibility of this method, a standard addition method was used to determine the concentration of cholesterol in 100-fold dilution of human serum. The recovery test was from 98.4% to 101.5%, and the RSD was satisfactory within 1.1% from the results shown in Table 2. These results illustrate that the proposed ratiometric fluorescence method has a great potential for detecting cholesterol in biological samples.

In summary, CeO2-CDs and CDs were successfully prepared by a simple one-pot hydrothermal method in this work. The CeO2-CDs had higher peroxide-like activity than CeO2 and the CDs had good fluorescence. Using the enzyme catalytic activity of CeO2-CDs and the fluorescence of CDs, a ratiometric fluorescence method for H2O2 and cholesterol was established. The determination ranges for H2O2 and cholesterol were 1.67 µM-2.01 mM and 1.66 µM-1.65 mM with the detection limits of 0.35 µM and 0.49 µM (3Ϭ), respectively. In addition, this method was successfully used to detect cholesterol in human serum, the results indicated it had a good application prospect in clinical.

Zhouping Yang: Data curation, Writing - original draft. Yang Liu: Data curation, Writing - original draft. Changfang Lu: Data curation, Writing - original draft. Guizhou Yue: Formal analysis. Yanying Wang: Formal analysis. Hanbing Rao: Writing - review & editing. Wei Zhang: Formal analysis. Zhiwei Lu: Writing - review & editing. Xianxiang Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Funding.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by a grant from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21305097).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: In situ growth of surfactant-free gold nanoparticles on nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for electrochemical detection of hydrogen peroxide in biological environments

Zhang, Gold nanoclusters as switch-off fluorescent probe for detection of uric acid based on the inner filter effect of hydrogen peroxide-mediated enlargement of gold nanoparticles, Biosens. Bioelectron., 91 (2017) 734‐740.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Red emission B, N, S-co-doped carbon dots for colorimetric and fluorescent dual mode detection of Fe3+ ions in complex biological fluids and living cells

Interfaces, 9 (2017) 12663‐12672.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: B-doped carbon quantum dots as a sensitive fluorescence probe for hydrogen peroxide and glucose detection
